TextENGNew: ...Losing consciousness was a first for me.
So waking up in a bed afterward -- something you hear about all the time -- was also a first.
``...Hello...?@k@vS05/10/101000024. Are you all right...?``
Noticing I had come to, a young doctor spoke to me.
I hadn't meant to respond, but my body language must have conveyed that I was conscious.
My head was wrapped in bandages.
The scratches covering my body had all been treated.
...As consciousness returned, so did the pain, and I groaned at the agony radiating through every part of me.

``Please don't try to get up. Just stay lying down.@k@vS05/10/101000026. It seems you took quite a beating to the head...@k@vS05/10/101000027. I'd like you to stay rested so we can monitor your condition for at least the next 24 hours.``
``...Where... am I?``
``This is my clinic.@k@vS05/10/101000029. I'm Irie. Pleased to meet you.``
...I realized this young doctor was the same one we'd passed in the oncoming car.
``...Is anyone... here?@k@vS05/13/101300561. ...Where's Ooishi-san...``
``Ah, I'll go get him.@k@vS05/10/101000031. Smoking isn't allowed inside the clinic, so I had him step outside.``
Dr. Irie left the room to fetch Ooishi.
...The scenery through the window was steeped in twilight gold.
The downpour had stopped, and the evening cicadas sang their cooling refrain.
I thought the higurashi's song was... comforting.
Before long, heavy footsteps -- unmistakably Ooishi's -- drew closer,@kand my fog-shrouded mind gradually reconnected with reality...
What had happened after I blacked out?
What had happened to the grandson?
changeReason: Japanese '孫' specifically means 'grandson'; original translates as generic 'boy' while new correctly uses 'grandson.'

TextENGNew: What about the reinforcements from Tokyo?
The hospital room door swung open, and Dr. Irie entered with Ooishi.
``So there's no danger to his life, right?``
changeReason: Japanese '命に別状' means 'danger to his life'; new translation 'no danger to his life' is more accurate than original's vaguer 'no serious damage.'

TextENGNew: ``...I can't guarantee that.@k@vS05/10/101000033. Head injuries are too dangerous to assess by appearance alone.``
changeReason: Old says doctor 'assessed wound was dangerous when saw it'; new says 'head injuries too dangerous to assess by appearance.' Different framing of same medical concern.

TextENGNew: ``Got it, got it.@k@vS05/11/100700605. I'd like to have a word with him in private, if that's all right?``
``...Of course. Call me if anything happens.``
Ooishi half-forcibly shooed Dr. Irie out and closed the door.
``How are you feeling?``
``...I did things I'm not used to, so my whole body is wrecked.``
``Nahahaha...``
Ooishi grabbed a folding chair leaning against the wall and sat down beside the bed.
``Were the culprits apprehended?``

``Unfortunately, no.@k@vS05/11/100700609. ...They've either fled the prefecture or are being hidden by the villagers.@k@vS05/11/100700610. Well, we won't let them get away.@k@vS05/11/100700611. Nfu fu fu fu!``
``And the minister's grandson?``

``Safe and in our custody at the station.@k@vS05/11/100700613. Though your colleagues from Tokyo apparently warned us not to take any statements.@k@vS05/11/100700614. Shige-chan was quite indignant about it. Na ha ha ha...``
Just as the kidnapping had been kept secret, the grandson's recovery would be too.
How this case would be handled from here, how it would be buried, how it would be made to disappear -- that was for the higher-ups to decide.
...If the case could be closed without the public ever catching wind, then our job was done.
...Though the possibility that the minister had negotiated with the kidnappers couldn't be ruled out.
...At this point, that was no longer my concern.@kMy role in this case had reached its conclusion.
``...Sorry for the trouble.@k@vS05/13/101300566. ...You must think Public Security is a real nuisance, don't you?``
changeReason: Japanese '公安' translated as 'Public Safety' in original vs 'Public Security' in new -- refers to different organizational names in English.

TextENGNew: ``Not at all.@k@vS05/11/100700616. We all draw our pay from the same government.``
Ooishi laughed heartily.
When we first met, I had instinctively disliked Ooishi, but now that feeling had vanished entirely.
``Honestly, it'd be more convenient for us if this case were handled openly.@k@vS05/11/100700618. Then we could go after the Sonozaki family, the real masterminds.``
``But the moment we start poking around, the pressure will come raining down.@k@vS05/11/100700620. ...So it goes.``
``Are you going to keep fighting the Sonozaki main house after this?``
``Nah.@k@vS05/11/100700622. The dark rumors never stop swirling, but I don't hold a personal grudge against them.@k@vS05/11/100700623. I'm just a humble civil servant, doing his job.``
Ooishi laughed so cheerfully that I couldn't help but join in.
``Oh, that's right.@k@vS05/11/100700626. There was word at the station from the prefectural police earlier.``
``Your colleagues have arrived in Nagoya and are driving here now.@k@vS05/11/100700628. You'll be relieved of duty as a wounded hero and handed over to them.``
``So that's how it is. ...Good work.@k@vS05/11/100700630. Your assignment in Okinomiya is officially concluded.``
Recalled to Tokyo.
...Those words drew an involuntary sigh of relief.
``...I'm deeply grateful for your cooperation, Ooishi-san.@k@vS05/13/101300569. ...It seems the money I paid you wasn't wasted after all.``
``Na ha ha ha ha!!@k@vS05/11/100700632. ...That was meant to be a joke, you know.``
Ooishi pulled out his wallet, casually withdrew several ten-thousand-yen notes, and stuffed them into my shirt's breast pocket.
changeReason: Japanese '壱萬円札' (ten-thousand-yen notes) is specific; original generalizes to 'large bills' while new preserves the specific denomination.

TextENGNew: ``This isn't a refund on the cooperation fee, mind you.@k@vS05/11/100700634. Consider it a personal gift from me to you.@k@vS05/11/100700635. The local sake around here is quite good, you know.@k@vS05/11/100700636. Use it to buy some as a souvenir for when you go back to Tokyo.``
Ooishi flashed me a grin.
``If your injuries aren't too bad, give me a call before you head back to Tokyo.@k@vS05/11/100700638. The old man and Sato-san have both been dying for a rematch.``
``...Oh my... ahahaha, I can't say no to that.``
``And no tricks this time.@k@vS05/11/100700640. Let's just enjoy a good clean game.``
``Easy for you to say.@k@vS05/13/101300572. An active-duty officer who can cheat that skillfully -- the world really is going to pieces.``
changeReason: Japanese 'よく言いますよ' is a retort meaning 'easy for you to say'; original mistranslates as 'They always say that.'

TextENGNew: ``Na ha ha ha ha ha ha!@k@vS05/11/100700642. It's not often we find someone who can play at our level or above.@k@vS05/11/100700643. It's a shame you're going back to Tokyo.``
changeReason: Both say the same thing about it being a shame Akasaka's going back to Tokyo.

TextENGNew: Hearing Ooishi say 'going back to Tokyo' drove home once more that I was released from my mission.
Yukie's due date was approaching.
...The delivery I had once given up on attending might still be within reach.
The thought made the pain in my wounds irrelevant -- I just wanted to hurry back to Tokyo and see Yukie's face.

``How about pretending your injuries are a bit worse and hanging around Okinomiya for a while?@k@vS05/11/100700645. I'll introduce you to some great spots.@k@vS05/11/100700646. And of course, I won't ask you to foot the bill.@k@vS05/11/100700647. Na ha ha ha!``
``I appreciate the offer, but I'll have to take a rain check.@k@vS05/13/101300574. My wife's due date is coming up.@k@vS05/13/101300575. I want to be there.``
``Oh my, oh my!@k@vS05/11/100700649. I had no idea!@k@vS05/11/100700650. Then you need to get home right away!``
After that, Ooishi spent some time grilling me about my wife in great detail, teasing and ribbing me mercilessly.
``...Well then.@k@vS05/11/100700653. How are you feeling?@k@vS05/11/100700654. Think you can get up?``
``Yes, I'm feeling much better.@k@vS05/13/101300577. I can stand and walk just fine.``
I stood up and shuffled around, which seemed to amuse Ooishi.
``I figured you wouldn't feel comfortable resting in a Hinamizawa hospital.@k@vS05/11/100700656. Dr. Irie wanted you to stay overnight.``
``...But the culprits are still at large.@k@vS05/11/100700658. Technically, this is still enemy territory for you.``
``You're saying this hospital isn't safe?``
``Na ha ha ha.@k@vS05/11/100700660. ...Well, Dr. Irie has a certain standing in the community.@k@vS05/11/100700661. I don't think anyone would try anything here, but still.``
...I had relaxed a little now that things had settled, but Ooishi was right.
Being reminded that this was enemy territory made me suddenly uncomfortable.
With nothing left to do but leave, the odds of anyone harming me were rationally very low.
But low odds didn't mean safe.
``...You're right.@k@vS05/13/101300580. If all I'm going to do is lie down, I might as well do it on a hotel bed.``
``Exactly.@k@vS05/11/100700663. I think that's for the best too.``
Just then, I caught a glimpse of the clock over Ooishi's shoulder.
It was already evening.
...The window for calling Yukie's hospital was about to close.
I wanted to tell her as soon as possible that I'd be back at her side.
``I'm going to call Yukie's hospital.@k@vS05/13/101300582. Is there a payphone in the lobby?``

``Na ha ha ha!@k@vS05/11/100700665. Newlyweds are so diligent about these things.@k@vS05/11/100700666. I'm pretty sure there's one by the register.@k@vS05/11/100700667. I'll wait here, so go on, go on.``
changeReason: Japanese '会計の脇' means 'by the register/reception'; original correctly says 'by the register' while new changes to 'in the lobby.'

TextENGNew: Ooishi, laughing about how jealous he was, opened the window and pulled out a cigarette -- inside a hospital room, no less.
Grateful for his consideration, I left the room and headed for the lobby payphone.
The lobby was just a short distance down the hall from my room.
For a village this remote, the clinic was impressively well-equipped, though it was nothing compared to the general hospital where Yukie was staying.
Running through a hospital wasn't exactly good manners, but with calling hours about to end, I couldn't help myself.
The lobby was deserted.
Perhaps because of the hour, even the reception desk was unmanned.
...Well, that might actually be fortunate.
I wasn't planning to say anything embarrassing, but having someone overhear my conversation with Yukie in this quiet space would be awkward.
A quick look around, and I spotted the payphone immediately.
Digging through my pockets, I found a handful of hundred-yen coins.
Calling Tokyo from here, I'd burn through them in no time.
I picked up the receiver, fed in a coin, and dialed the number I now knew by heart -- Yukie's hospital.
Click-click-click-click.@kClick-click-click.@kClick-click-click-click-click.
changeReason: Sound effect difference: original 'Whirrr' vs new 'Click-click-click' for Japanese rotary dial onomatopoeia 'ジーーーコロコロコロ'; new better captures the mechanical clicking.

TextENGNew: Yukie's hospital number had a lot of eights and nines, which made the rotary dial painfully slow.
Normally it wouldn't bother me, but at a time like this, even that small delay was maddening.
...But the vague sense that something was wrong had been there since I started dialing.
How to put it... the receiver was too quiet.
That characteristic dial tone -- the steady hum of a live line -- was absent.
Something felt off. There was no response from the phone at all. I hung up to reset.
The coin clattered back into the return slot.
I reinserted it and dialed again, but the receiver remained dead silent.
``...What is this?@k@vS05/13/101300585. ...Is the line disconnected...?``
Every second wasted here was a second less with Yukie...@kI felt an irrational urgency building.
``Excuse me~~~!``
I sensed someone on the other side of the window and called out.
A man who looked like a pharmacist appeared almost immediately.
``Yes, what can I do for you?``
``Sorry to bother you.@k@vS05/13/101300588. This phone seems to be malfunctioning.@k@vS05/13/101300589. ...Could you take a look?``
The man came over and tried various things, pressing the receiver to his ear.
...Come to think of it, he worked at the hospital, not the phone company.
He probably knew as little about phones as I did.
``...Huh?@k@vS05/00/manC00006. What's this...?``
The man held up the cord connecting the phone to the wall.
...It had been cleanly severed.

``What on earth?@k@vS05/00/manC00008. Well, this is a problem...@k@vS05/00/manC00009. It won't work until someone from the phone company comes to fix it...@k@vS05/00/manC00010. If you need a phone, would you like to use the one in the office?``
I didn't want to make a call from the hospital office with people watching...
``Is there another payphone nearby?``

``Let's see... if you go out and follow the road, you'll reach the shopping street.@k@vS05/00/manC00012. I think there's one at a tobacco shop on one of the corners.@k@vS05/00/manC00013. You should be able to find it if you head that way.``
changeReason: New translation omits the last part about finding the payphone easily, which is present in the Japanese.

TextENGNew: I was dressed in a hospital gown.
Sandals on my feet.@kHead wrapped in bandages -- the unmistakable look of a hospital patient.
No one would say I was dressed for going outside.@kBut youth has its privileges.
Choosing the chance to talk to Yukie even a moment longer, I dashed outside just as I was.
Follow the road to the shopping street.
I'd find it right away.
Armed with only those two instructions, I bolted out of the clinic gates.
This village, nestled in a valley, grew dark rapidly once the sun began to set.
...The sparse streetlights were just beginning to glow, enough to attract moths.
The higurashi, knowing their chorus would soon end for the day, sang all the more poignantly...
The payphone wasn't as easy to find as the hospital staff had suggested.
Was it still up ahead? Or had I already passed it, getting farther away with every step?
Just as that anxiety threatened to crush me, I spotted a small shop on a corner with a sign reading 'Tobacco.'
changeReason: Japanese 'たばこ' literally means 'tobacco'; original translates the sign as 'Cigarettes' while new uses 'Tobacco.'

TextENGNew: I ran toward it, elated.
...Just as I'd been told, next to the tobacco shop's counter sat an old-fashioned payphone.
Without a watch, I didn't know the exact time, but... I probably didn't have much left.
But it was enough.@kJust enough to tell Yukie that my work was done and I'd be home soon.
I grabbed the receiver and slid a coin into the slot.
...But the same wrongness was there, identical to the hospital phone.
Even after inserting the coin, the phone gave no response at all.
I hung up and tried inserting the coin again.@k...But still nothing.
This phone was dead.
It couldn't be...
I traced the cord extending from the phone like a tail.
...It slipped through my fingers far too easily, ending in a cleanly severed stump.
...What... was this?
Having it happen twice, my bewilderment was now joined by a distinctly unpleasant feeling.
The cord's cut surface looked as though it had been sliced cleanly with a sharp blade.
It definitely hadn't frayed from age.
changeReason: Original 'didn't look like much time had passed since it was cut' misreads the Japanese '経年劣化で擦り切れた' (frayed from age/deterioration); new correctly says 'hadn't frayed from age.'

TextENGNew: I opened the tobacco shop's service window and searched the dim interior for someone.
Eventually, the sound of someone plodding down stairs, and an old woman appeared.
Her glacially slow pace somehow only made me more anxious.
``Need some cigarettes?@k@vS05/00/oldD00002. I'll have to get my husband...``
``I'm not here to buy cigarettes.@k@vS05/13/101300595. It's this phone here...``
I was about to ask her to look at the broken phone, but realized that would accomplish nothing and swallowed the words.
``Sorry, this phone seems to be broken. Could you tell me where the nearest other payphone is?``
The old woman chewed on my words slowly, as if they needed to be physically digested before their meaning could register.
``...The phone is broken?@k@vS05/00/oldD00004. That's a pickle...@k@vS05/00/oldD00005. There should be a phone booth down the road a ways.``
The old woman directed me to another payphone that was apparently quite a distance away.
Her explanation was so unhurried that I found myself growing impatient in spite of myself.
Just then, I spotted a clock in the dim interior of the shop.
The minute hand was nearing twelve.
Even if a working phone were right here, there might only be enough time to say 'goodnight' to Yukie.
And I was now heading for a phone booth at an unknown distance.
...Any sane person would give up for today. That should have been the natural conclusion.
But at that moment, I had become... stubborn, for lack of a better word.
Thwarted at the hospital, coming all the way out here and thwarted again.@k...I had undeniably become fixated on making this call.
``Thank you very much...@k@vS05/13/101300598. I'll try to find that phone booth.``
Declining her offer to draw me a map, I left the tobacco shop.
According to the old woman, the phone booth was far but the way was straightforward.
Follow this road straight, and I'd eventually see it.
Sandals slapping as I ran, I couldn't help but laugh at myself.
I'd admit it -- I had been diligent about calling Yukie.
But there had been days when I was too busy or too tired to call.
Today of all days, I was covered in wounds, exhausted, and nearly out of time.@k...It should have been perfectly reasonable to skip a day.
And yet, having been thwarted twice, had I become irrationally fixated?
Like a dog being teased with a treat dangled just out of reach?
``...What am I even doing?@k@vS05/13/101300600. ...Ahahaha.``
Even so, I couldn't extinguish the smoldering unease in my chest.
The hospital phone. The tobacco shop phone.
Neither cord had frayed from years of wear.
The clean cross-sections made it obvious that someone had deliberately cut them with a sharp implement.
Who? And why?
The more I thought about it, the more unsettled I became, so I forced myself to stop thinking...
...How far had I run?
The scenery around me was no longer a shopping street -- it was now a lonely stretch of road with barely even a house in sight.
Darkness had already settled in.
I was clearly, unmistakably, far too far from the hospital for a 'quick phone call.'
I recalled the clock at the tobacco shop.@k...It was probably almost time.
I had been running for a while, and my breathing was ragged.
...If I'd wanted so badly to hear Yukie's voice, I should have just swallowed my embarrassment and used the office phone.@k...A twinge of regret.
So... was it time to give up...?
...Just as I thought that.@k...I spotted a light ahead.
I had finally found it.@k...A phone booth.
The fluorescent light inside had apparently been replaced recently -- it shone with a crisp, reassuring white glow.
Though in the gloom of Hinamizawa, it served as little more than a salon for moths.
But regardless -- I had finally found it!
As my hand touched the phone booth door, I realized I was gasping for breath, sweat pouring from every pore.
...I didn't know what time it was, but... there might still be the slimmest chance of being put through to Yukie.
Having come this far with this much effort, turning back without even trying would be unthinkable.
Before picking up the receiver... another wave of anxiety crested.
The fear that this phone, too, might have its cord cut.
What about this phone booth's wiring?
I crouched down and traced the bundle of cables as they ran along the corner up to the ceiling.
At a glance... nothing appeared to be wrong.
I ran my fingers along the wiring as far as I could reach.
...They came away with nothing but a layer of gritty dust.@kNothing out of the ordinary.
...Was there some kind of trend in Hinamizawa of cutting payphone cords as a prank?
I should mention this to Ooishi-san later.
Even if it was just mischief, it was still destruction of public property.
...In any case, this one was fine. This time.
I pulled a coin from my pocket with one hand and lifted the receiver with the other.
...But this time, even before bringing the receiver to my ear, something felt wrong.
The receiver was... light.
...More than unpleasant -- almost sickening.
To identify the source of my dread, I slowly... fearfully... looked at the receiver in my hand.
``...What the hell... is this?``
...The coiled cord connecting the receiver to the phone unit... had been cleanly severed, its frayed end dangling limply.
The unease I had been desperately suppressing clawed its way up my spine.@k...It was a kind of... fear.
Then... the crunch of gravel underfoot.
Inside the lit phone booth, the light reflecting off the glass made it impossible to see into the darkness outside.
I steeled myself... and stepped out.
The interior of the booth had grown warm from the strong light.
Just stepping outside, a wave of cool air washed over me.
And there... stood a silhouette.
A small silhouette.
The figure's long hair danced softly in the cool breeze.
``...An injured person shouldn't be sneaking out of the hospital...``
Somewhere in my mind, I had known this girl would appear.
Without any rational basis, I knew that she was that girl.
``...Right, Akasaka?``
She -- Furude Rika -- spoke as if admonishing me...
``...Did you... cut the phone cords?``
Her expression didn't change, and she gave no answer.
In other words, no response.
...And yet, I felt as though she had confirmed it.
``...Why would you do something like this...?@k@vS05/13/101300606. ...Why?!``
The last word came out sharper than I intended.
I hastily tried to soften it, but the girl didn't seem frightened in the least.
``...It's already too late.``
I interpreted her words to mean that calling hours at the hospital were over.
...But on reflection, that was strange.
How would she know something like that?
And yet, I couldn't take her words any other way.
The girl stood perfectly still, like a doll, not moving a muscle.
...Were it not for the occasional breeze stirring her long hair, I might have believed time itself had stopped.
``...I can see quite clearly that you find me unsettling, Akasaka.``
``Huh... ah... no... that's not true...``
My denial rolled out like a scripted line.@kBut her observation was dead on target.
I had always felt something ominous and uncanny about this girl.
...Ever since those strange words at the shrine... the feeling had never left.
``...Hee hee.``
She seemed to notice that my expression contradicted my words, and laughed at the discrepancy.
``...Akasaka, you coward.@k@vS05/27/100500127. Hee hee hee...``
``I-I'm not a coward...!``
Being laughed at by a child this small didn't sit well with me.@kSo I retorted.
``...Even if you find another phone, it's too late now.``
She didn't need to tell me.
I had clearly missed the window.
Calling hours were probably already over by the time I reached this phone booth.
I was too late. I had no choice but to give up for today.
Once I accepted that, my manic fixation on finding a phone evaporated.
...Even I found it strange how obsessive I had been.
With that strange compulsion gone, I could only look back at myself with a rueful laugh.
After all, the state I was in... was far from normal.
A hospital gown and sandals.
Head wrapped in bandages, drenched in sweat from head to toe.
It was a wonder the old woman at the tobacco shop hadn't been alarmed.
Running around town at twilight in this getup, driven by the simple desire to hear my wife's voice...@k...the folly of youth, or just plain embarrassing.
...In any case, all I could do was laugh at myself.
``...Now.@k@vS05/27/100500130. There is nothing left for Akasaka to do tonight.``
``You're right, nothing at all.@k@vS05/13/101300613. ...I'll go quietly back to the hospital.``
``...That's for the best.@k@vS05/27/100500132. Do you know the way back?``
``...I'd like to think so.``
I gave the girl a small wave and started heading back the way I came.
The girl fell into step beside me.
Looking at her face, I saw a mischievous smile.
...As if she were deeply curious about whether I'd get lost.
``Is Rika-chan's house also in this direction...?``
``Won't your parents be angry that you're walking around alone at this hour?``
``Not really.@k@vS05/27/100500135. My parents are busy today, so they've probably forgotten all about me.``
``...Even so, you should head home.@k@vS05/13/101300618. They'll be worried.``
``...Even if they are, talking with Akasaka is much more interesting, so I'm fine.``
She said that with a carefree smile, then looked up at me and smiled again.
When she showed me that smile... the eerie feeling surrounding her faded.
...But even so, she remained a profoundly mysterious girl.
We arrived at the turn I had taken earlier.
...But it was much darker now than before, and with everything reversed on the return trip, I wasn't confident this was the right road.
No, I was sure... this was the right corner.
As I was about to step forward, the girl grabbed my clothes.
``...Isn't Akasaka going back to the hospital...?``
``Of course... that's what I'm doing.@k@vS05/13/101300620. ...Did I take the wrong turn...?``
A brief silence.
Then, still holding onto my clothes, the girl began to walk.
And so began our modest stroll together -- this mysterious girl and I, walking back toward the hospital.
